rows.imp_rat: Inadequate differential after a utility interruption directly loses overpressure protection (relevance 7); its duration determines how long the zone is exposed to ingress (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Redundant utilities make prolonged inadequate differential uncommon, though brief dips occur. Duration of inadequate differential after utility loss depends on backup response speed, which UPS/redundancy shortens substantially. Recovery is largely automated, needing little operator skill. Backup power and redundant utilities are a meaningful capital cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of sterilant leakage from the reservoir after replenishment
rows.plain: Prevent the disinfecting chemical from leaking out of its container after it is refilled. This protects people, equipment, and the surrounding area.
rows.imp_rat: Sterilant leakage from the reservoir after replenishment introduces hazardous chemical exposure and can compromise decontamination dosing; relevant to safe operation with high severity given the toxicity of sterilants like hydrogen peroxide vapor.
rows.sat_rat: Sealed connectors, O-rings and post-fill leak checks make leaks uncommon under standard practice, though a residual rate of missed reseating errors persists. Replenishment and reservoir reconnection add modest time to a service visit but rarely delay the maintenance window. Correct reservoir sealing depends on a trained technician following the OEM procedure, and torque/seating errors still occur with less experienced staff. Leak-related spills waste sterilant and can force decontamination reruns, but the material cost per event is limited.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator exposure to hazardous product residues or decontamination agents during interior cleaning and decontamination
rows.plain: Protect workers from dangerous leftover product or cleaning chemicals while they clean the inside of the enclosed work area. This reduces the chance of harmful exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Operator exposure to product residues or decontamination agents during interior cleaning is central to protecting personnel who routinely perform this task. Severity is high given repeated close contact with both hazardous residues and caustic agents.
rows.sat_rat: Established pre-cleaning decontamination and glove-port access substantially reduce exposure, but manual wipe-down and aggressive agents leave a recurring residual exposure risk. Automated vapor-phase decontamination cycles run before manual cleaning add substantial but scheduled cycle time. Interior cleaning through gloveports or via full entry demands careful, trained technique and disciplined agent handling to avoid direct contact. Decontamination agents, PPE and downtime are meaningful recurring costs of every interior clean.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between operator recognition of the boundary breach and containment response activation
rows.plain: Start containing the breach as soon as the operator notices it. A faster response can limit the spread or effects of the problem.
rows.imp_rat: Delay between recognizing a boundary breach and activating containment prolongs uncontrolled release; timeliness is important to limiting the breach (high relevance) and longer delay proportionally increases exposure severity (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Automated activation is fairly consistent, though the manual recognition step means response times vary between shifts and operators. Modern isolators use automated pressure and alarm systems that trigger containment responses within seconds of a detected breach, but operator recognition and manual escalation still introduce lag. Fast response depends on well-trained operators recognizing what an alarm means and reacting decisively, so skill gaps still slow the loop. The monitoring and interlock hardware needed for rapid activation is already embedded in qualified isolators, so no extra spend is incurred per event.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between onset and recognition of an adverse isolation trend
rows.plain: Shorten the time between signs that isolation is getting worse and someone noticing it. This helps people respond before contamination spreads further.
rows.imp_rat: Time from onset to recognition of an adverse isolation trend affects early intervention for the CFJ (relevance 7); slow recognition lets a trend become a breach (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Continuous data capture makes trends recognizable, but reliance on periodic human review means slow trends are often recognized late. Trend software and alarms shorten recognition of adverse trends, though threshold-based alarms lag on slow drifts. Distinguishing a genuine adverse trend from noise still needs experienced analyst attention. Trending tools are standard and their cost is modest relative to benefit.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which the impact of contamination ingress on sterile-product safety and sterility is underestimated
rows.plain: Avoid understating how much an unwanted entry of germs could threaten the safety and cleanliness of the product. This helps people take contamination risks seriously.
rows.imp_rat: Underestimating the impact of ingress on product safety distorts risk-based control decisions (relevance 6); underestimation can lead to inadequate defenses and released non-sterile product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Mature QRM frameworks and sterility-assurance principles make gross underestimation uncommon, though subtle underestimation of rare pathways still slips through. Assessing contamination impact on sterility is embedded in established risk-assessment routines that are not unusually time-constrained. Understanding how ingress propagates to sterility failure requires microbiological and process expertise that is available but concentrated in specialists. Underestimating impact can cause recalls or patient harm, an enormous latent cost the status quo only partially guards against.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the severity of a barrier excursion is underestimated
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the seriousness of a failure in a protective barrier is judged too lightly. This helps people take the right steps to protect the product and workers.
rows.imp_rat: Underestimating excursion severity leads to inadequate response decisions for the CFJ (relevance 6); underreaction can leave contamination unaddressed (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Severity is frequently misjudged because the status quo offers little objective grading, making this unreliable. Severity assessment happens under time pressure, often too quickly or too slowly. Correctly gauging excursion severity is a demanding expert judgement with little decision support. Underestimation risks releasing contaminated product, a high-cost error.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of worst-case isolator states and interventions excluded from acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Make sure the approval rules include the most difficult equipment conditions and the ways people may need to intervene. This helps show that the equipment remains acceptable in challenging situations.
rows.imp_rat: Worst-case states and interventions excluded from acceptance criteria leave the hardest conditions unqualified for the CFJ (relevance 6); the untested worst case can fail in production (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Worst-case interventions are often under-represented in acceptance criteria, a known regulatory-finding gap. Enumerating worst-case states and interventions is time-consuming during protocol development. Identifying which worst-case interventions to include is heavily expert-dependent and frequently incomplete. Broadening acceptance coverage raises protocol and execution costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of contamination-risk scenarios omitted from isolation evaluation
rows.plain: Consider every situation that could cause contamination when checking whether the work area and equipment keep contamination contained. This helps prevent overlooked risks.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted risk scenarios from isolation evaluation leave blind spots in the CFJ defense (relevance 7); an unassessed scenario can produce an unanticipated breach (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: QRM covers common scenarios well but omitted edge cases remain a real recurring gap. Scenario enumeration is an upfront risk-assessment activity that rarely constrains the schedule. Deciding which risk scenarios to evaluate depends on experienced facilitators anticipating non-obvious upsets. Missing scenarios cause costly late findings, but the evaluation itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of aseptic interventions omitted from barrier-control requirements
rows.plain: Make sure every step involving germ-free handling has the required protective barriers. This reduces the chance that the work will become contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Aseptic interventions omitted from barrier-control requirements leave common handling risks uncontrolled (relevance 7); an uncontrolled intervention is a likely ingress event (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Intervention lists in APS help, but some interventions are still omitted from barrier requirements. Enumerating interventions for control requirements is bounded upfront work. Identifying every aseptic intervention needing controls relies on experienced process knowledge. Omitted interventions cause costly deviations later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator exposure to hazardous material while activating the containment response
rows.plain: Protect operators from dangerous material while they start the response to contain a breach. They should be able to act without being exposed.
rows.imp_rat: Operator exposure while activating the containment response is relevant but relates to an abnormal/emergency mode rather than routine containment, giving moderate relevance. Severity is high because exposure during a fault condition typically involves already-escaping hazardous material.
rows.sat_rat: Containment-response procedures for breaches are less standardized than routine operation, so exposure during activation is only partially mitigated and varies by facility. Activating a containment response during an upset takes time during which exposure risk persists. Operators must recognize the upset and execute the correct response under stress, demanding judgement and drilled training. Emergency response provisions add modest cost but spill kits and PPE are inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that excursion-response actions further destabilize the barrier condition
rows.plain: Make sure actions taken after a problem with the protective enclosure do not make its condition less stable. This helps prevent the response from increasing the risk to the product.
rows.imp_rat: Excursion-response actions that further destabilize the barrier worsen an active CFJ failure (relevance 6); destabilizing recovery can escalate contamination (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Response actions sometimes worsen conditions because reactions are improvised, so this remains unreliable. Poorly chosen actions can add corrective cycles and time. Avoiding destabilizing actions requires deep understanding of barrier dynamics. Destabilizing responses can escalate to full-batch loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of unintended barrier door or access-panel openings during production
rows.plain: Have fewer accidental openings of protective doors or access panels while production is running. This helps keep the work area protected from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Unintended door/access-panel openings are gross breaches of the physical barrier (relevance 8); they expose the zone directly to the room and can contaminate product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Access-panel interlocks make unintended openings during production genuinely rare and well controlled. Interlocked doors respond instantly to prevent openings, so no delay is involved. Physical interlocks remove reliance on operators remembering procedure, cutting the skill demand sharply. Interlock hardware adds modest cost that is standard in isolator builds.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of pressure-regime excursions outside qualified limits during routine use
rows.plain: Have fewer times when air-pressure conditions go outside the approved limits during normal use. This helps keep the process in a safe, dependable state.
rows.imp_rat: Pressure-regime excursions beyond qualified limits during routine use recurrently threaten the overpressure CFJ (relevance 7); repeated excursions accumulate ingress risk to product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Automated pressure regulation keeps routine-use excursions relatively infrequent. Continuous monitoring flags excursions promptly during routine use. Routine pressure control needs limited operator skill once tuned. Control systems are already in place, so marginal cost is low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that protective-control testing compromises isolator containment
rows.plain: Make sure testing the safety controls does not break the sealed barrier that keeps harmful material inside. This protects people and the surrounding area during testing.
rows.imp_rat: Testing that itself breaches containment introduces contamination during a routine assurance activity; relevance is high because it corrupts the very safety verification, though such events are bounded to test windows and often detectable.
rows.sat_rat: Most protective-control tests are designed to run without compromising the barrier, so containment loss during testing is uncommon under standard procedure. Control testing is normally done without breaching containment and adds little time. Following validated test sequences requires moderate skill to avoid inadvertently opening the barrier. Minimal consumables make this a low-cost activity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of leaking seals, ports, gloves, or penetrations left undetected during commissioning testing
rows.plain: Find more leaks in seals, connection points, gloves, and openings during testing before the equipment is put into use. This helps ensure the enclosure keeps harmful material contained.
rows.imp_rat: Leaking seals, ports, or gloves left undetected during commissioning are central to establishing initial containment integrity; consequences are serious but caught earlier in the lifecycle before hazardous operation, slightly limiting severity.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure-decay and tracer methods catch most gross leaks, but small or intermittent penetrations still slip through commissioning and surface in service, leaving a real residual gap. Commissioning leak tests (pressure decay, tracer gas) are established procedures but sweeping every seal, port and glove takes considerable bench time. Reliable leak detection still leans on experienced technicians who know where marginal leaks hide and how to interpret ambiguous decay curves. Standard test rigs and consumables are affordable, though re-testing after remediation adds recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the incorrect containment zone is activated after the breach
rows.plain: Make sure the response activates the correct area for containing a leak or breach. This helps keep the hazard from spreading to the wrong place.
rows.imp_rat: Activating the wrong containment zone after a breach means the response fails to contain the actual hazard; highly consequential when it occurs since the breach remains unaddressed, but conditional on a breach event already happening.
rows.sat_rat: Predefined zone mapping usually activates the right containment area, though misconfiguration or overlapping zones can occasionally misdirect the response. Zone activation is triggered near-instantly by the control logic once a breach is classified. Correct zone selection is programmed into interlock logic rather than left to operator choice, lowering skill dependence. Zoning logic is part of the fixed control system, so correct activation adds no marginal cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the selected immediate action does not match the barrier-excursion condition
rows.plain: Choose an immediate response that fits the specific problem with the protective enclosure. The right response can limit the effect of the problem and protect the process.
rows.imp_rat: Immediate action not matching the excursion condition means an ineffective response to a CFJ threat (relevance 6); a mismatched action can leave isolation compromised (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Action-condition mismatch is common because response depends on human judgement rather than guided decision aids. Selecting the matching action adds decision time during response. Matching action to condition depends on operator expertise and recall of procedures. Wrong actions cause rework and batch loss cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical aseptic-zone states absent from barrier-integrity assessment
rows.plain: Leave out fewer important conditions in the protected production area when checking whether its barriers are working properly. This helps reveal possible breaks in protection.
rows.imp_rat: Critical aseptic-zone states absent from barrier-integrity assessment leave gaps in qualification of the CFJ (relevance 6); an unassessed critical state can breach unexpectedly (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Critical states are commonly missed from integrity assessment, leaving recurring coverage gaps. Mapping all critical aseptic-zone states extends the assessment planning phase. Deciding which states are critical demands deep process knowledge that is unevenly available. More states assessed means more test runs and cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of difference between qualification conditions and the most challenging permitted isolator conditions
rows.plain: Test the sealed work enclosure under conditions that are as difficult as any situation allowed for its use. This provides stronger evidence that it will protect the work when conditions are challenging.
rows.imp_rat: Difference between qualification conditions and worst permitted conditions leaves the hardest cases unassured for the CFJ (relevance 6); the unqualified worst case can fail in production (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: A gap between qualification and worst-case permitted conditions commonly remains, undermining representativeness. Recreating most-challenging conditions in qualification takes extra setup time. Judging and reproducing worst-case conditions requires strong process understanding. Simulating extreme conditions can require costly rigs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of maintenance technicians being exposed to hazardous residues retained by exhausted filtration media
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that maintenance workers come into contact with harmful substances trapped in used air filters. This helps protect them while they remove and replace the filters.
rows.imp_rat: Maintenance technician exposure to residues in exhausted filter media is a well-known containment hazard, central to protecting service personnel. Severity is high due to concentrated hazardous accumulation, though it affects a narrower maintenance window.
rows.sat_rat: Combining BIBO change-out with pre-decon reliably limits technician exposure, though the retained residue on spent media remains an inherently hazardous variable. Pre-decontamination cycles plus donning PPE add time before filters can be safely handled, but this is a routine planned duration. Recognizing residual-hazard risk and executing safe-change with full PPE requires trained, attentive technicians familiar with the specific compound. Respiratory protection, suits and decontamination agents are recurring costs that are nonetheless standard for this task.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between barrier controls and identified contamination risks
rows.plain: Make sure the protective measures match the ways contamination could occur. This helps avoid weak protection against known risks.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between barrier controls and identified risks means controls don't address real threats to the CFJ (relevance 7); the gap can leave actual pathways unmitigated (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: CCS traceability reduces mismatch, yet subtle mismatches persist as a recurring gap. Aligning controls to risks is a design-review task done off the critical path. Judging mismatch between controls and risks requires expert cross-checking that is not uniformly rigorous. Mismatches found late are costly, but the review is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that certified isolator conditions leave qualified limits before the production run begins
rows.plain: Make sure the sealed work area is still within its approved condition limits when production is about to start. This helps prevent a run from beginning in an unsuitable environment.
rows.imp_rat: Certified conditions drifting out of qualified limits before the run means the job starts unassured (relevance 7); starting outside limits risks the entire batch's sterility (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Certified conditions usually hold to run start, though drift between certification and start occasionally occurs. Pre-run certification checks verify conditions shortly before start, limiting drift time. Certification relies on qualified personnel interpreting readiness data. Requalification and monitoring costs are budgeted routine expenses.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that the isolator is used outside its approved qualification state
rows.plain: Spend as little time as possible using the sealed work area when it is outside its approved condition. This helps limit the risk of work being done in an unverified state.
rows.imp_rat: Using the isolator outside its approved qualification state operates the CFJ without valid assurance (relevance 6); product made in that state may be non-sterile and non-compliant (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Formal qualification tracking usually catches lapses, but drift between requalification intervals can go unrecognized for a time. Requalification schedules and change-control gates limit how long an out-of-qualification state persists once flagged. Judging qualification status requires QA expertise to interpret validation boundaries. Running out of qualification risks regulatory action and batch rejection costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable contamination-control requirements omitted from the isolation criteria
rows.plain: Make sure none of the relevant rules for preventing contamination are left out of the requirements for the sealed work area. This helps ensure the area meets all applicable rules.
rows.imp_rat: Applicable contamination-control requirements omitted from isolation criteria leave the CFJ under-specified (relevance 7); a missing requirement can leave a real pathway uncontrolled (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Standard requirement libraries help, but applicable requirements are still occasionally omitted from criteria. Requirement capture is front-loaded and does not delay operations. Knowing all applicable contamination-control requirements requires specialist regulatory and engineering knowledge. Omitted requirements found late are costly to retrofit.

rows.source_job: Decontaminate enclosure interior before initial use
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that enclosure contamination remains above acceptance limits after pre-use decontamination
rows.plain: Make sure the inside of the enclosure is clean enough after its first cleaning and treatment before the machine is used. Any remaining contamination should be below the allowed level.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination remaining above acceptance limits after pre-use decontamination undermines the clean-start requirement central to sterile work; consequences are cross-contamination of subsequent operations, serious but pre-operational.
rows.sat_rat: Validated decon protocols and acceptance sampling work well for known contaminants but residual hot-spots above limits still slip past sampling, leaving recurring gaps. Decontamination and swab/verification cycles are time-consuming, often requiring wait periods and repeat cleaning. Effective decontamination and correct sampling depend on trained personnel following validated protocols precisely. Decon agents, consumables and analytical sampling add recurring cost, and re-cleaning multiplies it.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminants entering the chamber with items transferred through the airlock
rows.plain: Keep dirt or other unwanted material from entering the enclosed work area when items are moved through the transfer opening. This helps protect the items and the work area from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminants entering via airlock transfers is a recurring breach path relevant to maintaining the clean interior; consequences compromise the protected atmosphere but are typically contained within the box rather than released outward.
rows.sat_rat: Established transfer protocols catch most contamination, but incomplete purges and human shortcuts still let contaminants enter. Airlock purge and cycle procedures add waiting time before items can be transferred in. Proper transfer discipline is procedural and trainable but relies on operator adherence to wipe-down and cycling steps. Airlocks and consumable wipes are standard budgeted costs of routine operation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to hazardous residues while servicing air-handling components
rows.plain: Protect the technician from dangerous leftover material while working on the parts that move or control air. This reduces the chance of harmful exposure during servicing.
rows.imp_rat: Exposure to hazardous residues while servicing air-handling components is a maintenance-specific risk peripheral to routine operation (moderate relevance) but carries serious toxic-exposure consequences when it happens (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Decontamination reaches primary surfaces reliably but ductwork and plenum internals can retain residues that pre-cleaning does not fully address, leaving a recurring exposure risk. Air-handling service often requires full decontamination and access, extending the maintenance window. Servicing fans, ducts and plenums with residual hazard demands experienced technicians and careful PPE discipline. PPE, decontamination and downtime for HVAC access are notable recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of credible loss-of-isolation scenarios omitted from the contamination risk assessment
rows.plain: Make sure the contamination review includes every believable way that the sealed area could lose its protection. This helps people prepare for problems before they happen.
rows.imp_rat: Loss-of-isolation scenarios omitted from the risk assessment leave breach modes unanalyzed (relevance 6); an omitted scenario can occur unmitigated in service (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: FMEA and risk-workshop practice covers many scenarios, yet completeness is never assured and rare scenarios are routinely missed. Building the loss-of-isolation scenario set is embedded in existing FMEA-style routines without heavy time burden. Imagining credible failure scenarios requires experienced practitioners, and completeness tracks their skill. An overlooked scenario can lead to expensive contamination excursions, but scenario workshops are cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of unintended air leakage through barrier interfaces
rows.plain: Prevent unwanted air from escaping or entering where protective barriers meet. This helps keep the intended air pressure and clean conditions in place.
rows.imp_rat: Unintended air leakage through interfaces erodes the barrier and overpressure integrity (relevance 8); leakage volume correlates with ingress risk but small amounts may be tolerated (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Isolator leak-tightness standards and integrity testing are mature, so unintended leakage is well controlled though never zero. Leak testing is a defined qualification step, but chasing intermittent interface leaks can extend commissioning. Locating and sealing barrier-interface leaks demands experienced technicians and careful gasket/glove management. Glove and gasket integrity programs are a recurring consumable and labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contaminants into the isolator during air-handling service
rows.plain: Prevent dirt or other unwanted material from entering the sealed work area while its air-handling parts are being serviced.
rows.imp_rat: Introducing contaminants during air-handling service risks sterility of subsequent operation, relevant since air handling defines the clean environment (moderate-high relevance), though effects are usually caught by revalidation before production (moderate-high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Opening air-handling components inherently exposes the system to ingress, and there is no strong engineering control to guarantee cleanliness during service, so outcomes vary with practice. Re-establishing clean conditions and requalifying after air-handling service adds substantial time. Preventing contamination ingress during HVAC service depends heavily on technician aseptic discipline and controlled work practices. Post-service cleaning, filters and requalification are meaningful recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminants escaping the isolator during removal and disposal of exhausted filtration media
rows.plain: Keep harmful particles from escaping the enclosed work area while used air-filter material is removed and discarded.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminants escaping during removal/disposal of exhausted filters is a recognized containment failure mode, so relevance is high though it occurs at end-of-life rather than routine operation. Severity is substantial given accumulated hazardous load in spent media.
rows.sat_rat: Safe-change bag-in/bag-out technology is a mature, widely deployed engineering control that reliably contains filter media, though bag tears or technique lapses occasionally breach containment. Bag-in/bag-out (BIBO) filter housings and safe-change procedures are established practice that take moderate time but are well-rehearsed. Trained maintenance technicians follow validated safe-change SOPs, but proper bag manipulation still demands careful manual technique and vigilance. BIBO housings, disposable bags and PPE are recurring consumables but are a standard, budgeted part of isolator upkeep.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between onset of isolation degradation and escalation for a production decision
rows.plain: Shorten the time between a loss of protection and raising the issue for a production decision. This helps the team decide quickly whether production should continue or change.
rows.imp_rat: Time from isolation degradation onset to escalation for a decision affects timely production hold (relevance 6); slow escalation can let compromised production continue (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Defined escalation SOPs help, but reliance on human recognition and hand-offs makes timely escalation inconsistent. Escalation pathways exist but depend on manual review cadence, introducing delay before a production decision is triggered. Deciding when degradation warrants escalation requires seasoned judgement. Delayed escalation extends exposure and inflates eventual remediation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of barrier interfaces and penetrations excluded from integrity assessment
rows.plain: Make sure every place where a protective barrier is joined or penetrated is included in the check of whether it remains intact. This helps prevent weak or overlooked spots from allowing contamination through.
rows.imp_rat: Barrier interfaces/penetrations excluded from integrity assessment leave breach-prone points unverified for the CFJ (relevance 6); an unassessed interface can fail undetected (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Interfaces and penetrations are frequently the leak points and some are still omitted from formal integrity assessment. Testing every interface and penetration lengthens qualification appreciably. Identifying all penetrations for assessment requires thorough design review skill. Comprehensive interface testing adds tooling and labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an access-point closure losing its seal after contact with a handled item
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that an opening will lose its seal after a worker touches or moves something through it. This helps keep the inside protected from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: An access-point closure losing its seal after item contact creates a latent breach in the barrier (relevance 7); an unnoticed unsealed closure allows ongoing ingress (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Interlocked closures generally reseal reliably, though contact-induced seal loss occasionally slips through unnoticed. Reseating a closure adds negligible time. Ensuring reseal after contact is a modest operator attention item. Seal-failure consequences carry contamination cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of the enclosure boundary omitted from the pre-use integrity check
rows.plain: Check all of the enclosure’s edges before use so no part of it is missed. This helps confirm that it can safely contain what is inside.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting portions of the boundary from the pre-use integrity check is relevant because gaps in verification allow undetected breaches, but it is a test-coverage metric rather than a containment failure itself. Severity is notable since an unchecked region can hide a real leak that later exposes operators.
rows.sat_rat: Global pressure tests inherently exercise the full boundary, but localized methods and complex geometries mean some boundary sections are routinely under-checked with no systematic assurance of complete coverage. Ensuring every boundary segment is exercised takes extra planning time but is not a schedule driver. Whether the whole boundary is covered depends heavily on the engineer's understanding of the enclosure's geometry, making omissions an experience-dependent error. Comprehensive coverage costs little beyond additional test-point setup time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of inward airflow during an aseptic-zone intervention or transfer
rows.plain: Air should flow inward for as little time as possible when people enter the protected area or move items through it. This helps keep the area protected from outside contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Inward airflow during interventions or transfers reverses the protective cascade of the CFJ (relevance 7); its duration governs how much ambient contamination can enter (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: RTP and airlock designs contain inward airflow well, though interventions remain a recognized weak point. During transfers, inward airflow windows are kept short by procedural sequencing but still last as long as the transfer. Proper transfer technique depends on trained operators following the sequence correctly. Rapid-transfer ports reduce exposure at added equipment cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of barrier penetrations lacking contamination-control requirements
rows.plain: Reduce the number of openings through protective barriers that do not have stated requirements for preventing contamination. This helps stop dirt or germs from entering through those openings.
rows.imp_rat: Barrier penetrations lacking contamination-control requirements leave known physical breach points unmanaged (relevance 7); an uncontrolled penetration is a direct ingress route (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Penetration schedules and integrity testing reliably catch most missing controls. Cataloguing penetrations and their controls is a bounded design task. Ensuring each penetration has controls is fairly systematic once a penetration schedule exists. Uncontrolled penetrations cause costly integrity failures, but documentation is cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of modular enclosure interfaces assembled with compromised seals
rows.plain: Reduce the number of assembled enclosure connections whose seals are damaged or not properly closed. This helps keep the enclosed area protected from leaks or contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Modular enclosure interfaces assembled with compromised seals introduces built-in leak paths relevant to containment; the defect is serious but typically surfaces in subsequent integrity testing before hazardous use.
rows.sat_rat: Documented assembly procedures and torque specs cover most joints, yet compromised gaskets and uneven seating still recur, so the outcome is only acceptably consistent. Assembling modular interfaces with gaskets is routine and reasonably fast during preparation. Achieving a compliant seal across modular joints depends on correct torque sequence and gasket placement, which relies on trained assemblers. Gaskets and fasteners are cheap, but a compromised interface discovered late can force disassembly and rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of aseptic interventions deviating from approved isolator conditions
rows.plain: Reduce how often workers handle sterile materials in ways that do not follow the approved conditions inside the sealed clean enclosure. This helps keep the process safe and compliant with required rules.
rows.imp_rat: Interventions deviating from approved conditions introduce unqualified handling into the CFJ (relevance 6); deviations can breach isolation in uncontrolled ways (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: SOPs and training reduce deviations, but ad-hoc operator behavior under production pressure still produces recurring departures. Approved intervention procedures are timed and rehearsed, limiting duration variability. Adherence to approved conditions depends on operator discipline and supervisory oversight, which vary. Deviating interventions trigger costly investigations and possible batch loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the source or location of a barrier excursion is identified incorrectly
rows.plain: The source and location of a breach in the protective barrier should be identified correctly. This helps workers fix the right problem and protect the area again.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect identification of a breach source/location misdirects corrective action for the CFJ (relevance 6); misdiagnosis can leave the true breach open (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Source and location identification is frequently wrong given limited localized sensing, making this a chronic weakness. Locating a breach source consumes valuable response time. Correctly identifying breach location requires diagnostic skill that is often lacking in the moment. Misidentified sources prolong exposure and increase loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of aseptic-zone pressure or airflow deviation from validated limits before filling
rows.plain: Keep pressure and airflow in the sterile work area as close as possible to the approved safe limits before filling begins. This helps protect the product from contamination during filling.
rows.imp_rat: Pressure/airflow deviation from validated limits before filling means starting the job outside qualified isolation (relevance 7); it risks contaminating the entire subsequent run (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Pre-start pressure and airflow are tightly validated and gated, so deviations before filling are reliably caught. Pre-filling verification of pressure and airflow is a quick automated readiness check. Setpoint verification is largely instrument-driven, needing little operator judgement. Sensor and control infrastructure cost is already committed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that an access point remains unsecured during item handling
rows.plain: Keep an opening unsecured for as little time as possible while items are being handled. This reduces the chance that unwanted air, dirt, or germs can enter.
rows.imp_rat: Duration an access point remains unsecured during handling is a direct open-breach window in the barrier (relevance 7); longer unsecured time increases ingress exposure (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Access points are usually secured quickly by design, though manual steps sometimes leave them open longer than ideal. The need is to shorten unsecured-access duration, and interlocks and quick-close ports keep this short. Automated interlocks reduce reliance on operators to secure promptly. Interlock hardware is an accepted fixed cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of credible upset and recovery conditions excluded from the isolation criteria
rows.plain: Make sure the isolation rules cover every realistic failure and recovery situation. This helps keep problems from spreading beyond the area that is being isolated.
rows.imp_rat: Upset/recovery conditions excluded from isolation criteria leave failure modes unqualified for the CFJ (relevance 6); an unaddressed upset can breach isolation in service (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Upset scenarios are inconsistently covered, leaving recurring gaps in criteria. Considering upset and recovery conditions is upfront analysis work. Anticipating credible upset/recovery scenarios demands seasoned engineering judgement often in short supply. Excluded conditions found late drive costly changes.

rows.source_job: Replace vacuum-path filtration and trap components
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a vacuum leak at a disturbed seal or connection after component replacement
rows.plain: Prevent air from leaking through a seal or connection that was disturbed when parts were replaced. A leak could keep the equipment from working properly or allow contamination.
rows.imp_rat: A vacuum leak at a disturbed seal after component replacement prevents reaching vacuum, directly impairing the core function (relevance high); it is a maintenance-introduced fault usually caught in verification, so severity is moderate-high.
rows.sat_rat: Post-service leak testing is established practice and catches most disturbed-seal leaks, yet slow virtual leaks and marginal connections still slip through periodically. A helium or pressure-decay leak check adds meaningful time to every seal disturbance but is a normal part of the job. Correctly reseating seals and interpreting leak-test results demands trained hands and judgement that not every maintenance tech has. Leak-detection equipment and consumables are moderate ongoing costs but widely amortized.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of microbial contamination entering through sterile-to-nonsterile contact inside the isolator
rows.plain: Prevent germs from passing from an unclean area into the clean product area inside the enclosed work chamber. This helps keep the product safe from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Microbial contamination via sterile-to-nonsterile contact matters mainly for aseptic applications, so relevance to general containment is moderate. Severity is meaningful because compromised sterility can ruin batches or endanger patients downstream.
rows.sat_rat: Even with unidirectional airflow and grade-A interiors, inadvertent contact contamination remains a recurring deviation source only partly mitigated by SOPs. Maintaining aseptic separation slows manipulations but timing is not the primary loss. Avoiding sterile-to-nonsterile contact demands sustained aseptic-technique vigilance, one of the hardest skills to guarantee inside a confined isolator. Failures trigger costly investigations and discarded batches, though the preventive practice itself is low material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of containment leak paths at joints between assembled chamber sections
rows.plain: The joined sections of the chamber should not leave openings where air or contaminants can leak through. This helps keep the chamber sealed.
rows.imp_rat: Leak paths at joints between chamber sections are a specific structural contributor to containment loss, relevant but one of several leak mechanisms. Severity is significant as sustained micro-leaks can expose the environment over time.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure-decay and leak-rate testing reliably surface joint leaks, though residual leak paths at complex interfaces still recur and need rechasing. Joint leak testing at assembled sections is a defined step but pinpointing and resealing leak paths can add iterative time. Assembling gasketed chamber sections leak-tight relies on careful fitter workmanship and torque discipline that varies by installer. Rework to reseal joints wastes labor and sometimes gaskets, but costs are contained relative to full requalification.

rows.source_job: Install purification media and filtration components
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of atmosphere bypass caused by incompletely seated filtration components
rows.plain: Filters and other cleaning parts should fit fully so that air or other flow does not go around them. This helps ensure the flow is properly cleaned.
rows.imp_rat: Atmosphere bypass from incompletely seated filtration lets unfiltered air pass, undermining the containment/HEPA function; consequences are release of unfiltered hazardous aerosols, though usually a partial rather than gross breach.
rows.sat_rat: Scan testing and gel-seal designs make properly seated filtration verifiable, but incomplete seating causing bypass still occurs and is not always caught before use. Installing and seating filters (HEPA housings) is a standard, moderately quick preparation task. Correct seating and gel/clamp engagement demand attentive installers, as bypass paths are not visually obvious. Filter housings and media are a real material cost, and a bypass-forced replacement adds expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of representative isolator states omitted from pressure verification
rows.plain: Check pressure in all important conditions of the sealed work chamber, rather than leaving some conditions unchecked. This helps show that the chamber stays under the intended pressure control.
rows.imp_rat: Representative isolator states omitted from pressure verification leave the overpressure CFJ partly unverified (relevance 6); an unverified state can fail undetected in use (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Common operating states are usually verified but some representative states are still omitted. Covering all representative states in pressure verification lengthens testing. Selecting representative states is moderately expert-dependent. More states tested increases execution cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of differential excursions caused by airflow-resistance changes during sterile filling
rows.plain: Reduce how often pressure differences change because resistance to moving air changes during sterile filling. Stable air pressure helps protect the product from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Differential excursions from airflow-resistance changes during filling threaten the overpressure CFJ at the critical step (relevance 7); such excursions can permit ingress during filling (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Pressure control compensates for gradual resistance changes reliably, so excursions are infrequent. Airflow-resistance excursions are transient and controls recover quickly. Filter loading and control tuning are managed by engineering, not operator skill. Filter monitoring and control add recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of material containment being lost inside the protected zone during transfer
rows.plain: Keep materials contained while they are moved inside the protected clean area. This helps prevent contamination from entering or spreading.
rows.imp_rat: Loss of material containment inside the protected zone during transfer is a handling/spill risk within the barrier (relevance 6); it can contaminate the work area and product (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Enclosed transfer reduces gross containment loss, but spills and open-container mishandling inside the zone still occur with moderate frequency. Containment-loss cleanup interrupts handling but transfer itself is not time-critical here. Careful manipulation of open containers demands sustained operator dexterity. Spilled sterile material and rework represent real material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of damaging a glove, sleeve, or boundary seal during integrity testing
rows.plain: Avoid damaging gloves, sleeves, or protective seals while checking that they keep contaminants out. This helps protect both the worker and the product.
rows.imp_rat: Damaging a glove, sleeve, or seal during integrity testing is ironic self-inflicted damage to the barrier during a supporting QA task (moderate relevance); a created breach could compromise later containment if undetected (moderate-high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standardized test pressures and procedures keep test-induced damage uncommon, though aggressive or repeated testing still occasionally harms boundaries. Careful testing to avoid damage adds little time to the integrity check. Applying test pressures without over-stressing gloves or seals depends on operator technique and adherence to limits. An occasional glove or seal damaged during testing is a low-cost consumable replacement.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of glove and sleeve integrity losses that remain undetected during inspection
rows.plain: Find damaged gloves or sleeves during the inspection instead of leaving the damage unnoticed. This helps keep contaminants from reaching the product or worker.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected glove/sleeve integrity losses are a leading containment-breach pathway, so detection is central and severity is high when a leak goes unnoticed. Capped at the 5.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Visual inspection and intermittent testing routinely fail to catch pinholes and micro-leaks, so a meaningful fraction of integrity losses go undetected until damage is done. Manual glove leak inspection and periodic pressure-decay testing add appreciable time yet still miss defects between checks. Reliable detection of small integrity losses depends on inspector attentiveness and interpretation, making outcomes highly operator-dependent. Undetected breaches that surface later cause expensive batch rejections and investigations, a substantial hidden cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of protected-zone pressure outside its specified range during material transfer
rows.plain: Keep the protected area's air pressure within the required range during material movement, and shorten any time it falls outside that range.
rows.imp_rat: Protected-zone pressure outside range during material transfer weakens isolation during a known vulnerability window (relevance 7); it can permit ingress alongside the open transfer (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Pressure recovery during transfer is generally fast and monitored, though door/port cycling causes recurring brief deviations. Transfer-related pressure dips are transient and recover quickly through automated cascade control. Interlocked airlock sequencing reduces operator dependence during transfer. Airlock and interlock systems add cost but are standard equipment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to contained material during integrity testing
rows.plain: Protect the technician from contact with contained material while checking whether the gloves and sleeves are intact. This helps prevent exposure during the test.
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure during integrity testing is a specific test-phase scenario rather than the core production job (relevance moderate); when it occurs it means direct exposure to contained potent material, a severe health consequence (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Pre-test decontamination plus PPE reliably limits exposure during testing, though residual contamination on tested components remains a low-frequency risk. Integrity testing is typically done after a decontamination cycle, so protective sequencing adds routine time. Technicians follow validated test protocols with PPE, requiring moderate skill but well-supported by procedures. PPE and test consumables are modest recurring costs for this task.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that escaped purge gas creates oxygen-deficient conditions in the operator area
rows.plain: Prevent gas used to clear the enclosure from escaping and lowering the oxygen level where workers stand, which could make it unsafe to breathe.
rows.imp_rat: Escaped purge gas creating oxygen-deficient conditions is a narrower inert-gas edge case relative to the core containment job (relevance moderate); an oxygen-deficient atmosphere can asphyxiate operators, a potentially fatal consequence (severity very high).
rows.sat_rat: Inert-purge isolators are commonly fitted with room oxygen depletion monitors and alarms, making undetected oxygen-deficient conditions relatively rare where installed. Oxygen monitoring is continuous and does not impose delay on operation. Interpreting and responding to O2 alarms requires minimal specialized skill given standard alarms. Room O2 sensors and inert-gas management add modest capital and monitoring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of containment integrity being compromised during calibration activities
rows.plain: Keep the sealed area intact while pressure-measuring devices are adjusted, so nothing can leak out or get in.
rows.imp_rat: Compromising containment during calibration introduces a breach during a routine maintenance task; relevant to sustaining integrity but bounded in scope and typically performed under controlled, planned conditions.
rows.sat_rat: Most calibration is performed through sealed access points or externally, so containment compromise during calibration is uncommon. Calibration of sensors is usually done via ports or non-invasively, adding minimal time. Calibration technicians follow standard procedures that rarely require breaching containment, keeping skill demand routine. Calibration tooling and technician time are predictable low costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of aseptic-zone differential fluctuation caused by surrounding-area pressure changes
rows.plain: Keep the difference in air pressure between the protected area and nearby areas as steady as possible, even when conditions around it change. This helps stop less-clean air from entering.
rows.imp_rat: Differential fluctuation driven by surrounding-area pressure changes threatens the overpressure cascade (relevance 7); it can transiently permit ingress (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Cascade pressure control with airlocks is mature and keeps differential stable against most surrounding changes. Pressure control loops respond in real time so surrounding fluctuations are dampened without human intervention delay. Modern PID cascade control handles this automatically, requiring little operator skill once tuned. Robust control hardware and airlock buffering add cost but are standard in isolator builds.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that isolator containment leakage exceeds the defined acceptance limit during requalification
rows.plain: When checking the enclosed equipment again, the amount of material escaping from it should stay within the allowed limit. This helps keep harmful material contained.
rows.imp_rat: Leakage exceeding the acceptance limit during requalification is central to demonstrating ongoing containment fitness, giving solid relevance. Severity is meaningful but the failure is caught in a controlled test setting rather than during live hazardous processing.
rows.sat_rat: Automated pressure-decay leak testing against ISO 10648 class limits is a mature, repeatable method that reliably detects when leakage exceeds acceptance limits. Pressure-decay and leak-rate requalification tests are standardized and can be run in a defined test window. Automated leak-test instrumentation reduces reliance on operator judgement, though setup and interpretation still need a qualified technician. Test equipment and qualified-tester time are recurring but predictable requalification costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that facility power or pressurized gas remains live when isolator disconnection begins
rows.plain: Before disconnecting the enclosed equipment, the person should make sure that electricity and pressurized gas have been safely turned off. This prevents electric shock, sudden gas release, or unexpected equipment activity.
rows.imp_rat: Live facility power or pressurized gas at disconnection is a decommissioning safety error, peripheral to processing (moderate relevance) but capable of causing serious injury when it occurs (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: LOTO and permit systems make live power or pressurized gas at disconnection an uncommon, well-controlled event. Confirming power and gas are dead adds only brief verification time. Verifying zero energy is a routine, well-trained electrical/utility task. Verification uses inexpensive standard tools and labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of hazardous utilities remaining connected to the isolator when dismantling begins
rows.plain: Before taking apart the enclosed equipment, the person should make sure that no dangerous pipes, wires, or other supplies are still connected. This prevents unexpected exposure to danger during dismantling.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous utilities remaining connected when dismantling begins is a decommissioning-phase error, less central to core processing (moderate relevance), but if it occurs it can cause serious energy or material release (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Permit-to-work and disconnection checklists reliably ensure utilities are off before dismantling begins. Verifying all utilities are disconnected before dismantling is a quick checklist step. Utility disconnection is standard facilities work needing common, not scarce, skills. Disconnection incurs only routine labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of standing liquid remaining inside the enclosure at cycle start
rows.plain: Remove as much pooled liquid as possible from inside the enclosure before the treatment cycle begins. This helps the treatment start under the intended conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Standing liquid at cycle start impairs decontamination effectiveness underpinning the isolation baseline (relevance 6); pooled liquid can shield microbes and leave viable contamination (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Conditioning phases usually eliminate standing liquid, but cold spots and prior wet cleaning still leave occasional pooling. Pre-cycle conditioning to dry the enclosure adds some setup time before injection begins. Operators must visually confirm dryness, a manual check that depends on attention. Dehumidification uses modest energy with little material waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of aseptic-zone interfaces with undefined pressure relationships across specified states
rows.plain: Reduce the number of areas where it is unclear how air pressure should differ between connected clean areas under different operating conditions. This helps prevent unwanted air movement that could bring in contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Interfaces with undefined pressure relationships across states leave the overpressure cascade ambiguous (relevance 6); undefined relationships can permit unmanaged ingress paths (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Undefined pressure relationships at some interfaces persist, especially across less-common states. Defining pressure relationships for every interface across states is time-intensive. Specifying cascade relationships needs solid HVAC/isolator design knowledge. Documentation effort is the main incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of transient isolation-loss scenarios lacking defined acceptance and recovery boundaries
rows.plain: Reduce the number of brief situations where separation from contamination may be lost without clear rules for deciding whether the result is acceptable or how to recover. This helps people respond consistently when protection is interrupted.
rows.imp_rat: Transient isolation-loss scenarios lacking defined acceptance/recovery boundaries leave ambiguity in handling brief breaches (relevance 5); undefined boundaries can cause inconsistent, unsafe recovery decisions (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Transient isolation-loss scenarios frequently lack defined acceptance and recovery boundaries, a persistent weak area. Defining recovery boundaries for transient scenarios extends protocol work. Characterizing transient isolation-loss and recovery demands advanced expertise many teams lack. Modeling and testing transients adds notable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination entering the aseptic zone during barrier-integrity qualification
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that germs get into the clean working area while its protective enclosure is being tested. This keeps the test area clean and the results trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination during barrier-integrity qualification affects test integrity rather than live production isolation (relevance 6); it can invalidate qualification and mask a real defect (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: VHP decontamination and closed-isolator testing make contamination during qualification uncommon but not eliminated. Pre-qualification cleaning and decontamination cycles add time but are routine and scheduled. Preventing contamination during testing relies on disciplined aseptic technique and trained operators. Decontamination consumables and controlled setups add moderate recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the operator sustains a thermal burn while extracting a heated sample
rows.plain: Prevent the operator from being burned while taking out a hot sample. The sample must be handled in a way that protects the person taking it.
rows.imp_rat: A thermal burn during sample extraction is a serious injury consequence (severity), though it is a downstream handling event somewhat peripheral to the oven's core processing function (relevance).
rows.sat_rat: With hot samples and manual handling, burns remain a recurring risk that PPE mitigates but does not eliminate. Waiting for samples to cool or using tools adds handling time to each extraction. Burn avoidance still depends heavily on operator care, gloves, and technique rather than any engineered safeguard. PPE and heat-resistant gloves are cheap, so cost is a minor factor in this need.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that inspection handling damages a glove or sleeve assembly during active operation
rows.plain: Avoid tearing or damaging the attached gloves or protective sleeves while checking them during operation. This helps keep the operator and the work area protected.
rows.imp_rat: Inspection handling damaging a glove/sleeve during active operation can breach containment mid-process, a severe sterility event, though it is one specific damage pathway. Capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Even with SOPs, inspection handling still occasionally introduces micro-tears or weakens seams, so containment integrity during active operation is not consistently protected. Routine visual and pressure-decay glove inspections take only minutes, but doing them without snagging or over-flexing a glove during active operation still slows operators down as they work cautiously. Avoiding inspection-induced damage depends heavily on operator finesse and trained handling technique, since gloves are thin and vulnerable and there is little tooling to make careful handling foolproof. Accidental damage during inspection forces glove replacement, batch holds, and potential product loss, so the recurring cost exposure remains meaningful.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of serviced pumps, valves, or seals failing post-service containment and transfer-function checks
rows.plain: After repairs, make sure pumps, valves, and seals do not leak and still respond as expected. This helps the equipment contain materials and control their movement correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Serviced pumps, valves, or seals failing post-service containment checks is a maintenance-quality error moderately relevant to the job; the failing check catches the problem before return to service, limiting real-world severity.
rows.sat_rat: Standard acceptance checks catch gross faults, but a meaningful fraction of serviced components still fail later checks due to installation variability. Post-service functional checks on pumps, valves and seals extend turnaround but follow defined procedures. Proper reassembly and check execution demand skilled technicians, and component-specific expertise is unevenly distributed. Post-service failures force repeat teardown, wasted parts and downtime, only partly controlled by verification steps.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of loss of containment while the operator diagnoses or resolves an alarm or deviation
rows.plain: Prevent material or harmful substances from escaping while the operator investigates or fixes a warning or problem. The equipment should remain safely closed during this work.
rows.imp_rat: Loss of containment while diagnosing an alarm is directly tied to the core containment job and carries real exposure risk; capped here per batch ceiling though its live-breach nature keeps both proxies moderately high.
rows.sat_rat: Because resolution can involve interventions and door or transfer actions, containment is genuinely vulnerable while an alarm is being worked, and current practice does not fully protect it. Prolonged diagnosis extends the window during which containment is at risk. Maintaining containment while troubleshooting demands calm, trained operators who follow procedure under pressure. A containment loss during resolution can scrap a batch and force decontamination, a large cost hit.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of misclassifying observed deterioration or damage during containment boundary inspection
rows.plain: Make sure visible wear or damage to the protective enclosure is identified correctly. Accurate checks help prevent leaks or unsafe exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Misclassifying deterioration during containment boundary inspection is moderately relevant since accurate boundary assessment supports containment, but it is one step removed from live containment; severity is elevated because a misjudged breach could allow undetected hazardous exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Subjective acceptance criteria and inconsistent reference standards make misclassification a persistent, poorly controlled failure mode. Classifying findings adds little time but pauses for judgement calls do occur. Deciding whether observed damage is acceptable, monitor, or reject demands experienced judgement against often-vague criteria. Misclassification either scraps good parts or passes bad ones, both costly, but the act itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of decontaminating agent escaping from the isolator chamber into the surrounding work area
rows.plain: Prevent the chemical used for cleaning and removing contamination from escaping the enclosed chamber. This protects people working nearby from exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Decontaminating agent (e.g., VHP) escaping into the work area is a supporting-process concern rather than the primary containment job (relevance moderate); sterilant escape harms operators and can breach exposure limits, a meaningful but often reversible consequence (severity moderate-high).
rows.sat_rat: Sealed chambers with catalytic aeration and area H2O2 monitoring reliably keep agent escape below occupational limits during normal cycles. Aeration and monitored release are timed steps that do not materially delay the preparation stage beyond expectation. Setting up catalytic aeration and area sensors is largely engineered into the system, reducing reliance on operator judgement. Catalytic converters and area monitors add capital cost but are standard containment provisions.

rows.source_job: Vent chamber to safe opening pressure
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that chamber opening is attempted while residual vacuum remains
rows.plain: Prevent anyone from trying to open the chamber while air pressure inside is still too low. This helps avoid a sudden and dangerous opening.
rows.imp_rat: Attempting to open the chamber under residual vacuum is a real safety failure mode with injury potential (severity), and controlling it supports the job's safe unloading step (relevance), held within the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure-sensing door interlocks are common on quality ovens and reliably prevent opening under residual vacuum, though older units lack them. Vacuum-release valves and interlocks add only seconds before a door can be opened. Door interlocks reduce reliance on the operator remembering to verify vacuum is released. Interlock hardware is inexpensive and prevents damage from forced openings.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required aseptic activities incompatible with the isolation criteria
rows.plain: Use fewer sterile-handling tasks that conflict with the rules for keeping the area separated from contamination. This makes the work easier to carry out without weakening protection against contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Required aseptic activities incompatible with isolation criteria create operational conflict with the CFJ (relevance 6); the conflict can force isolation-compromising workarounds (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Ergonomic and process reviews catch most incompatibilities, but some surface only during operational readiness. Reconciling activities with isolation criteria is a design-phase iteration. Judging aseptic-activity compatibility requires combined process and barrier expertise that is hard to assemble. Incompatibilities found late force costly redesign or manual workarounds.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical pressure boundaries lacking verification evidence
rows.plain: Make sure there is proof that the pressure at every important boundary has been checked. This helps show that the areas are operating as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Pressure boundaries lacking verification evidence is largely a qualification-documentation gap for the CFJ (relevance 6); unverified boundaries could later prove inadequate (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Most critical pressure boundaries do get verified, though some secondary boundaries lack explicit evidence. Verifying each pressure boundary adds discrete test steps. Defining and evidencing pressure boundaries is fairly standardized engineering work. Additional verification points add limited instrumentation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation from validated temperature and vacuum control requirements after the firmware update
rows.plain: After updating the controller’s built-in software, keep temperature and vacuum within the already approved limits. This helps ensure the process still works as it was tested and approved.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation from validated temperature/vacuum control after firmware update can silently compromise all subsequent cycles (severity) and directly concerns core control accuracy (relevance), kept at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Firmware updates frequently introduce subtle control-parameter deviations, and status-quo re-validation is inconsistent, so deviations are a recurring surprise. Firmware flashing is quick but full re-validation of control behavior against requirements adds substantial time. Confirming that updated firmware still honors validated control logic requires specialized understanding of the controller and validation protocols that few field techs possess. The update itself is nearly free, though re-validation labor and possible downtime carry cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of renewed supporting-utility loss during recovery
rows.plain: Prevent the services needed to run the equipment from failing again while an interrupted operation is being restarted. This helps recovery continue smoothly.
rows.imp_rat: Repeated utility loss during recovery prolongs instability and complicates return to validated state; relevant to reliable restoration but a secondary, recovery-phase concern rather than the primary containment function.
rows.sat_rat: Backup power and utility redundancy reduce repeat failures, but cascading or common-cause utility faults during recovery still recur. Renewed utility loss during recovery restarts the clock, extending downtime unpredictably. Avoiding repeat loss relies on maintenance and facility staff diagnosing the root cause correctly under pressure. Redundant utilities and backups exist but represent significant capital, and repeated losses still drive rework cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which isolator pressure and airflow deviate from qualified limits at certification
rows.plain: Keep the chamber's pressure and air movement within the tested acceptable ranges during the readiness check. This shows that the protected work area is operating as expected.
rows.imp_rat: Pressure/airflow deviation from qualified limits at certification affects readiness assurance for the overpressure CFJ (relevance 6); certifying deviant conditions can authorize a compromised run (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Pressure and airflow at certification are consistently near setpoint with qualified control. Certification measurements are taken promptly against setpoints. Reading and judging deviation at certification uses standard qualified procedures. Calibrated instrumentation for certification is a standard cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of current isolator-use scenarios absent from lifecycle qualification evidence
rows.plain: Make sure the testing and supporting evidence covers every way the sealed work area is currently used. This helps show that each use is safe and works as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Current use scenarios absent from lifecycle qualification evidence leave real operating modes unqualified (relevance 5); the unqualified mode may perform inadequately in service (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Actual use scenarios often drift beyond documented lifecycle qualification evidence. Keeping lifecycle evidence current for all use scenarios is an ongoing time burden. Recognizing which current scenarios lack coverage requires lifecycle-management discipline. Ongoing requalification for evolving scenarios carries recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of hazardous fluid remaining in the vacuum-oven system after drainage
rows.plain: Leave as little dangerous liquid as possible in the heating-and-air-removal system after it is emptied. This reduces the risk during later work.
rows.imp_rat: Residual hazardous fluid after drainage poses exposure and contamination consequences (severity), but drainage is a maintenance-adjacent task rather than the core heating/vacuum job (relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Standard drain-and-flush practice leaves recurring pockets of trapped fluid in seals, traps and lines, so complete removal is inconsistently achieved. Draining a vacuum oven's fluid systems is a quick procedure, but verifying complete removal often requires additional flushing cycles that add time. Effective drainage depends on a technician knowing every low point and dead-leg in the system, which is expertise-dependent and not universally documented. Residual hazardous fluid drives up disposal cost and can force re-treatment, though the fluid volumes in an oven are typically modest.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that incoming materials introduce contamination into the isolator interior
rows.plain: Avoid bringing dirt or germs into the enclosed work area with incoming materials. This helps protect the product while it is being made.
rows.imp_rat: Incoming materials introducing contamination affects product integrity more than operator protection, making it moderately central to the containment CFJ. Severity is moderate as it degrades process/product rather than directly endangering people.
rows.sat_rat: Pass-through decontamination is standard but manual wipe-down variability means incoming materials still occasionally carry contamination inside. Decontamination cycles and material staging add dwell time before items can be introduced. Effective surface disinfection and wipe-down of incoming items depends heavily on consistent operator technique. Sporicidal agents, transfer disinfection and airlock decontamination consume ongoing consumables and time-based cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of ancillary equipment puncturing an isolator glove or sleeve during manipulation
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that supporting equipment will tear or puncture the protective glove or sleeve while someone is working inside the enclosed area. This matters because a breach could expose the product or the worker to harm.
rows.imp_rat: Ancillary equipment puncturing a glove or sleeve creates a breach in the primary barrier (relevance) with potentially serious operator exposure to hazardous agents (severity), constrained to the 6.0 maximum.
rows.sat_rat: Sharps and ancillary equipment punctures remain a common, hard-to-eliminate breach mode with only partial engineering controls available. A puncture is instantaneous and unpredictable, so speed of the operation is not the issue. Avoiding glove punctures depends on careful, attentive tool handling in a cramped space, a persistent operator-vigilance burden. Glove replacement and puncture-driven downtime impose recurring cost, and thicker puncture-resistant gloves reduce dexterity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of residual materials with unconfirmed identity before handling
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of leftover material whose identity has not been confirmed before anyone handles it. This helps prevent mix-ups and unsafe handling.
rows.imp_rat: Handling residual materials of unconfirmed identity is a cleaning/handling ambiguity somewhat peripheral to the containment job (lower relevance), but mishandling unknown residues can cause cross-contamination or exposure (moderate-high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Residues of unconfirmed identity are a chronic problem that routinely forces worst-case handling assumptions with little standard remedy. Identifying unknown residual materials requires analytical turnaround that delays handling. Confirming unknown residue identity depends on analytical chemistry expertise rarely embedded in disposal crews. Lab identification of unknowns adds significant testing cost per case.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of isolator acceptance criteria that cannot be objectively verified under specified qualification conditions
rows.plain: Make sure the requirements for the enclosed processing equipment can be checked with clear evidence under the stated test conditions. This matters because requirements that cannot be tested cannot be confirmed reliably.
rows.imp_rat: Acceptance criteria that cannot be objectively verified weaken qualification rigor for the CFJ (relevance 5); unverifiable criteria can let a marginal isolator pass (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Some acceptance criteria remain subjective or non-verifiable under specified conditions. Reworking non-verifiable criteria delays protocol approval. Writing objectively verifiable acceptance criteria is a specialized skill often done imperfectly. Revising criteria and re-testing adds cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unsupported assumptions used to set isolation criteria
rows.plain: Base the rules for keeping areas isolated on evidence instead of guesses that have not been supported. This helps ensure the protection is strong enough for the actual risks.
rows.imp_rat: Unsupported assumptions in setting isolation criteria weaken the basis of the CFJ's defenses (relevance 5); a wrong assumption can produce inadequate limits and latent risk (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Assumptions are often carried without formal challenge, leaving a recurring reliability gap. Documenting and challenging assumptions is analytical work off the critical path.
